intro:
The Babylonian Captivity refers to the period of Judah's exile in Babylon following Nebuchadnezzar II's destruction of Jerusalem. It unfolded in three deportations: 605 BC (including Daniel and companions — Dan. 1:1-4), 597 BC (including King Jehoiachin, Ezekiel, and approximately 10,000 leading citizens — 2 Kings 24:10-16), and 586 BC (the destruction of the city, the temple, and the deportation of the remainder — 2 Kings 25:1-12). Jeremiah had prophesied a seventy-year duration (Jer. 25:11-12; 29:10), a periodization cited in Daniel 9:2 and 2 Chronicles 36:21, which explains it as payment for the land's accumulated unpaid sabbatical years (Lev. 26:34-35).

The exile was the most catastrophic event in Israelite history — the simultaneous loss of land, temple, kingship, and national existence. It provoked profound theological reorientation: the prophets Ezekiel and the exilic Isaiah (chs. 40-55) reframed the catastrophe not as YHWH's defeat but as his righteous judgment on covenant betrayal, and announced a future restoration surpassing the first Exodus. The exile ended when Cyrus the Great of Persia conquered Babylon in 539 BC and issued his decree permitting exiled peoples to return to their homelands (Ezra 1:1-4; Isa. 44:28). The return under Zerubbabel, Ezra, and Nehemiah was experienced as partial fulfillment of the restoration prophecies. The synagogue as an institution for communal worship without a temple originated in the Babylonian diaspora.
